speaker: interviewer
text: November the 22nd. [INAUDIBLE]-- November 22, '94. Survivor Gertrude Weiss, interviewer Sandy Jacobson. Is the fifth tape.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about this picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture, the smaller picture on the book, is the very famous rabbi, Yechezkel Landau. He is known by the name of Noda ben Yehuda. It's his most famous work. He did a lot of [INAUDIBLE]. He was the rabbi of Prague for 40 years in the middle of 18th century. He was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] most prominent, the most prominent Jewish authority on business law and laws by which Jewish life is governed. And he's still taught today. There is a-- there is an anecdote that they say that there is no time of the day or night in the world where somewhere his work is not studied.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he was your ancestor?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was my ancestor. My grandfather, [? Basro ?] Landau, and he had the certificate which showed the-- the genealogy of he was a direct descendant of him. And we grew up with this knowledge that we have to be proud because-- and we have to behave a certain way because we are descendants of Noda ben Yehuda.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good. OK, tell us about this picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my father and mother. It's a picture which was taken when I was a young girl, and which is enlarged from a very small passport picture what we found in the rooms of our home in Satu Mare when I-- when we returned from concentration camp. And my mother's name was Landau, Chana Freida. My father was [? Yitsak ?] Feuer, Ignatz Feuer, in Hungarian. They lived in Satu Mare. My father was born in Satu Mare, as well as his father. And he was a grain merchant in Satu Mare.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when would you think this picture was probably taken? The early 1920s? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it might have been taken late 1920s.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, good. OK. Tell me about this.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my husband's mother. Her name was also Chana, interestingly. And she lived in [ROMANIAN], where my husband was born. And I knew her. I met her as a young child, as a young girl, when I was-- during summers, vacationing by my grandmother. I remember she was a very lively, capable woman. She was-- she was the strong one in the family, I think. She was stronger than her husband, who was a very fine but not as assertive as her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any idea when this picture would have been taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have no idea, but she is married. She is-- I don't know. Early 1900s, early 1920s I suppose.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my wedding picture. I don't remember how-- how it survived. I--

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, this picture was taken after the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, but I don't know, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: What year would it have been taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: We traveled so much. It had to be taken in the beginning of '46. And to the right of my husband, next to my husband, is my sister, my only sister who survived but who is not with us any longer, unfortunately. And next to me is my brother-in-law and sister-in-law. He-- my brother-in-law is the oldest brother of my husband who lived a few blocks from us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So and what was-- what is your husband's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband's name is Arian Weiss.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so this picture was taken in 1946. Where? In what town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Beginning of-- in Satu Mare.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Satu Mare.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's where we got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get your dress?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about this picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is my two older sons. My third son wasn't-- didn't arrive yet. It's Jerry, the younger one, and Peter.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I think they are adorable. It was taken in Los Angeles. Not in this home, in an apartment. And it was taken in the beginning of the '50s.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] .

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my three sons, our three sons. In the middle is Peter, and then the taller one next is Jerry, and the youngest one is Bobby. It was Jerry's bar mitzvah. As you see, they are-- handling the checks. They have difficulty in handling the bar mitzvah gifts.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was-- when would this have been?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when would this have been? In the early '60s.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and here in Los Angeles?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Tell me about these fine-looking young men.

speaker: interviewee
text: These are the four sons of Bobby, our youngest son. The oldest one in the middle is [INAUDIBLE] [? Levy, ?] Next to him standing is-- with the glasses, yes, because [INAUDIBLE] has no glasses, is [? Avi. ?] Then it's [? Dovid ?] And the little one is Yehuda, but he's called Huda, because he never could say Yehuda. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in their home in Pittsburgh.

speaker: interviewer
text: And about when?

speaker: interviewee
text: About when? That had to be taken about four years ago.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. OK, tell me about these little girls.

speaker: interviewee
text: These are the two little girls, our only granddaughters, of Bobby, our youngest son, who after four boys, presented us with two granddaughters. My husband was in seventh heaven. He-- he always wanted to have a daughter of his own and after six grandchildren, only grandsons, he was really heartbroken and desperate. And when these little girls came along, he was in seventh heaven.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what are their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Their name is-- is Sarah and Chana. They are--

speaker: interviewer
text: And Chana is named for your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They are-- they all have only Jewish names. When I grew up, it was customary I had a Yiddish name, and I had a non-Jewish name. But these children of my son's today is only-- only one name. And that's how--

speaker: interviewer
text: So when was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken one year ago.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Because Chana is--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, tell us about this, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of Jacqueline and her two sons. Jacqueline is the wife of Jerry, my middle son, whom I had the pleasure of introducing earlier, and his two sons. The older one, the dark one, is [? Michael ?] And the little one is [? Eli. ?] They-- they were just really unbelievable.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when-- how old--

speaker: interviewee
text: Adorable and gorgeous.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were they? When was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken about nine years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that would make it, like, 1985, '86?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. One more. Tell us, please, about this.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a serigraph done by the well-known artist Abecassis, which was commissioned by my son Jerry on the occasion of his son's, [? Michael's, ?] bar mitzvah. It was commissioned about 160 pieces because it was handed out in a very nice portfolio for every guest as a souvenir. This depicts [? Michael's ?] life, and the piece is titled And the back of the serigraph is the explanation of the different things which symbolize certain things in-- in-- in his life and Jewish life and-- and history. So I think it's something very nice and something, a beautiful memento of his bar mitzvah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.
